Search results for "llvmlite create_target_machine emit_object add_module example code snippet":

Title: Example—compiling a simple function — llvmlite 0.20.0 documentation
URL: https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/v0.20.0/user-guide/binding/examples.html
Content: * Example—compiling a simple function. Compile and execute the function defined in `ir-fpadd.py`. For more information on `ir-fpadd.py`, see Example—Defining a simple function. The function is compiled with no specific optimizations. from __future__ import print_function from ctypes import CFUNCTYPE, c_double importllvmlite.binding as llvm# All these initializations are required for code generation! The engine is reusable for an arbitrary number of modules. """ # Create a target machine representing the host target = llvm. create_target_machine() # And an execution engine with an empty backing module backing_mod = llvm. create_mcjit_compiler(backing_mod, target_machine) return engine def compile_ir(engine, llvm_ir): """ Compile the LLVM IR string with the given engine. The compiled module object is returned. """ # Create a LLVM module object from the IR mod = llvm. verify() # Now add the module and make sure it is ready for execution engine. finalize_object() return mod engine = create_execution_engine() mod = compile_ir(engine, llvm_ir)# Look up the function pointer (a Python int) func_ptr = engine.

Title: Target information - llvmlite - Read the Docs
URL: https://llvmlite.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user-guide/binding/target-information.html
Content: Target information allows you to inspect and modify aspects of the code generation, such as which CPU is targeted or what optimization level is desired. Minimal use of this module would be to create a `TargetMachine` for later use in code generation. from  llvmlite  import binding target = binding. :   Return a string representing the default target triple that LLVM is configured to produce code for. :   Return a target triple suitable for generating code for the current process. :   Get the object format for the given *triple* string, or the default triple if `None`. :   Get the name of the host’s CPU as a string. The returned value is an instance of the `FeatureMap` class, which adds a new method `.flatten()` for returning a string suitable for use as the `features` argument to `Target.create_target_machine()`. :   Create a new `Target` instance for the default platform that LLVM is configured to produce code for.

Title: llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit_object() creates incorrect ...
URL: https://github.com/numba/llvmlite/issues/181
Content: ## Navigation Menu. # Search code, repositories, users, issues, pull requests... # Provide feedback. We read every piece of feedback, and take your input very seriously. # Saved searches. ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. To see all available qualifiers, see our documentation. # llvmlite.binding.TargetMachine.emit\_object() creates incorrect executable type on Windows #181. ## Footer. I was messing around and trying to create an executable using llvmlite on my Windows machine, but I couldn't get it to work. I noticed that even though all of the default settings told me that the executable would be of the COFF format, it was an ELF executable. Here's an example shell run showing the issue:  . This generated file cannot be run by Windows. I'm running Windows 10, the beta that allows Bash on Windows, and Python 3.5 in a Conda Virtual Environment. I'm using the latest llvmlite version available on Conda. ### Type. ## Issue actions. ### Footer navigation.

Title: COS 320 LLVMlite Documentation - cs.Princeton
URL: https://www.cs.princeton.edu/courses/archive/spring19/cos320/hw/llvmlite.shtml
Content: LLVMlite is a small subset of the LLVM IR that we will be using throughout the course as the intermediate representation in our compiler. This document explains the structure of well-formed LLVMlite programs, the semantics of LLVMlite in terms of an abstract machine, and the relevant parts of the code provided with the assignments. To give you a sense of structure of LLVMlite programs and the most basic features, the following is our running example, the simple recursive factorial function written in the conrete syntax of the LLVMlite IR. The argument is prefixed with "%" to indicate that it's an identifier local to the function, while fac is prefixed with "@" to indicate that it is in scope in the entire compilation unit. Next, at (2) we have the first instruction of the body of fac, which performs a signed comparison of the argument %n and 0 and assigns the result to the temporary %1.

Title: Building and using llvmlite - a basic example - Eli Bendersky's website
URL: https://eli.thegreenplace.net/2015/building-and-using-llvmlite-a-basic-example
Content: llvmlite was created last year by the developers of Numba (a JIT compiler for scientific Python), and just recently replaced llvmpy as their bridge to LLVM. One of the reasons the Numba devs decided to ditch llvmpy in favor of a new approach is the biggest issue heavy users of LLVM as a library have - its incredible rate of API-breaking change. The LLVM C++ API is notoriously unstable and will remain this way for the foreseeable future. Since building the IR using repeated FFI calls to LLVM proved to be slow and error-prone in llvmpy, llvmlite re-implemented the IR builder in pure Python. This also reduces the "API surface" llvmlite uses, since the textual representation of LLVM IR is one of the more stable things in LLVM. LLVM has a great tool named llvm-config in its binary image, and the Makefile in llvmlite uses it, which means building llvmlite with any version of LLVM I want is just a simple matter of running:.
